data.name: Yahia Fofana
data.description: “In my all-time African XI, I’d have Yahia Fofana in goal” – those are the words of Fofana’s Côte d’Ivoire head coach, Emerse Faé. It’s a compliment that demonstrates the faith that Faé has in Fofana after just three years working together. The goalkeeper arrived in the international setup in 2023 and is a product of the Le Havre academy. He was named player of the season at Angers in 2024-25 before moving to Turkey. For Ivorians, his penalty save against Mali in the quarter-finals of the 2023 Africa Cup of Nations, which Côte d’Ivoire won on home soil, is his finest moment. Quick reflexes are the greatest strength of their first-choice goalkeeper.

data.name: Ousmane Diomandé
data.description: The composed centre-back was a revelation with his interceptions and intelligence in the back four when he arrived in the national setup in 2022, quickly winning a starting spot. He fell down the pecking order during the 2023 Africa Cup of Nations and is now third or fourth-choice in the middle of defence. Nevertheless, he’s a reliable member of the squad and will be ready if called upon in North America. The 22-year-old’s first experience of European football came with Mafra, a third-tier club in Lisbon. Now he’s at the top table in the Portuguese capital with Sporting and has already won the Portuguese league twice.

data.name: Ghislain Konan
data.description: Emerse Faé’s preferred option at left-back. Konan had been around the national setup since 2017 before he spent the second half of 2024 without a club and was dropped. But, after six months of little game time at Burgos in Spain, he has played his way back into the Côte d’Ivoire fold during a consistent season at Gil Vicente and was first-choice left-back at the last Africa Cup of Nations. Konan’s qualities are largely defensive, being a strong battler in his own third. Faé can count on his experience of playing in five countries on three continents throughout his club career, including spells at Vitória Guimarães, Stade Reims and Al-Nassr.

data.name: Jean Michaël Seri
data.description: The veteran provides balance to Emerse Faé’s midfield from his position in front of the back four, his experience in a defensive sense just as valuable as his calmness at bringing the ball out from the back. The 34-year-old has played club football across three continents during his career, notably in the Premier League with Fulham. When his dream move from Nice to Barcelona broke down in 2017, he was not best pleased: “I exploded. The walls shook,” he told Mundo Deportivo. “Nice had promised me one thing but they didn’t keep their word and asked for more money.” After winning his first cap in 2015, a year after Côte d’Ivoire’s last World Cup appearance, this tournament is likely to mark the final step on an international career that included the 2023 Africa Cup of Nations title.

data.name: Wilfried Singo
data.description: Singo is at home among modern full-backs. He’s quick, strong and good on the ball, as effective going forward as he is in his own defensive third. After winning his first Côte d’Ivoire cap in 2021, he spent a year out of international football for personal reasons before a return in 2023. Having missed the last Africa Cup of Nations through injury, he will renew his rivalry with Guela Doué, who impressed in his place in Morocco, for the right-back spot this summer. The 25-year-old can also operate as a centre-back in either a back three or a back four. Singo initially wanted to be a goalkeeper like his father, who played amateur football. “But when I saw my friends scoring goals and all that, I decided to become an outfield player,” he said while at Torino.

data.name: Seko Fofana
data.description: Fofana and Côte d’Ivoire have not always been a match made in heaven. After switching his allegiance from France in 2017, the box-to-box midfielder was absent from multiple squads in the years that followed until he played a starring role in the team that won the 2023 Africa Cup of Nations on home soil, starting in the final against Nigeria. Having lost his starting spot in recent times, playing only a bit-part role at Afcon 2025, the 31-year-old has put together a good enough run of form in the second half of the season at Porto – winning the league title – to warrant a place on the plane.

data.name: Odilon Kossounou
data.description: The 25-year-old has all the hallmarks of a modern centre-back: tall and strong, assured on the ball and aggressive without it. He has partnered Evan N’Dicka in the Côte d’Ivoire backline since 2024 and it is a rock-solid pairing – Emerse Faé’s side did not concede a single goal in 10 matches in World Cup qualifying. After winning titles in Belgium with Club Brugge and Germany with Bayer Leverkusen, last summer’s permanent move to Atalanta has given him the opportunity to improve on a different side of his game. “In Serie A it is very important to be awake tactically,” he has said.

data.name: Franck Kessié
data.description: The 29-year-old Côte d’Ivoire captain is arriving at his first World Cup. His first cap came shortly after Brazil 2014, the last time Les Éléphants reached the tournament. Kessié established himself in the national team in 2016 and has been a favourite of every coach since, winning his 100th cap in March. During the celebrations to commemorate this milestone, the president of the Ivorian federation, Idriss Diallo, laid down Kessié’s World Cup challenge: lead the team for at least six games, into the quarter-finals. Ivorian football’s poster boy has two African titles to his name, at under-17 and senior level, and has won the league in Italy and Spain. He left Barcelona for Saudi Arabia, where he is a double Asian Champions League champion.

data.name: Ange-Yoan Bonny
data.description: Previously uncapped and only naturalised a few weeks ago, the 22-year-old Inter striker is in contention to lead the line for Côte d’Ivoire at this World Cup. Emerse Faé will be hoping he can fit into his system as a lone striker having played alongside a partner for much of last campaign. Now a Serie A and Coppa Italia winner in his first season at Inter, scoring a handful of goals in all competitions along the way, Bonny knew he had to pass his French school exams to earn his move to Parma in Italy. “My mother threatened me: ‘Listen to me, Yoan, if you don’t pass your baccalauréat, I promise you we won’t sign the contract,’” he told France Football. Bonny never knew his father and was brought up by his mother, who bought him his first football shirt: an Inter one, with Samuel Eto’o’s name on the back.

data.name: Simon Adingra
data.description: Adingra has won his place back after being omitted from Emerse Faé’s squad for the Africa Cup of Nations last December. The 24-year-old former Brighton winger is creative and a sharp dribbler, and loves taking players on one-on-one. After falling victim to a scam targeting young footballers in Benin as a teenager, he landed in Europe with Nordsjælland. He was named best young player at Côte d’Ivoire’s victorious home Africa Cup of Nations in 2023 – setting up both goals in the final – but his career has not quite kicked on. An average return to the Premier League with Sunderland ended in January, when he was loaned to Monaco. He is out of contract this summer.

data.name: Yan Diomandé
data.description: The 19-year-old is certainly one to watch this summer. His form in Germany, where he has added goals to his game in his first season at the elite level, has made him a contender to win Tuttosport’s prestigious Golden Boy award, given to the best player under 21 in Europe. Diomande is an eye-catching dribbler and has tormented defenders in the Bundesliga since arriving from Leganés last summer for €20m. The big question is: can he handle the pressure of what comes next? If he can inspire Les Éléphants into the World Cup knockout stages and beyond, who’s to say one of Europe’s biggest clubs won’t come in with a mammoth offer?

data.name: Elye Wahi
data.description: Had his World Cup thrown into disarray after it was revealed that he was being investigated in connection to fixing with the Marseille prosecutor’s office saying that “a 23-year-old professional football player, competing in France’s Ligue 1, was arrested on 29 May 2026 as part of their investigation”. It was first reported that he had been denied entry to Canada for Côte d'Ivoire's second group game but in the end he was able to travel. The former France youth international turned down a Côte d’Ivoire call-up three years ago but a senior cap for Les Bleus failed to materialise. After bursting on to the scene in the Champions League with Lens, the striker has had a difficult couple of years with Marseille and Eintracht Frankfurt but he has found his shooting boots again on loan at Nice with seven goals since January, earning him another opportunity with Les Éléphants in March. “The people around me – my agent, my family – didn’t really want me to come to Nice,” Wahi told L’Équipe. “By signing for Nice in January, I was jumping into the wolf’s mouth, but I was sure of my decision.” Mobile and fast, he has the attributes to play as a loan No 9 or alongside a strike partner.

data.name: Christopher Opéri
data.description: Opéri travels to North America as a late replacement for Clément Akpa, who had to pull out of Emerse Faé’s squad owing to a thigh injury. Opéri, a 29-year-old left-back, made his international debut two years ago but hasn’t been able to establish himself. He was included in last December’s squad for the Africa Cup of Nations but featured in just one game – the last group game against Gabon. However, he has the sort of attributes you would expect from a modern full-back, knowing how to defend and get forward in equal measure. He can also deliver a high-quality cross into the box or pick a teammate out with a cutback. “There’s a positive pressure,” he has said about playing for Côte d’Ivoire. “With the supporters, you get the sense that there’s a real fervour behind us.” After winning promotion to Ligue 1 with Le Havre, Opéri has been in the Turkish capital since last summer.

data.name: Oumar Diakité
data.description: Diakité offers something different to most other players in this squad: pure power from a wide position. And he is an important presence off the field too as one of the younger voices in the dressing room who will keep spirits high – a must-have for any national team looking to go deep into a major tournament. His nickname, Wrou Wrou, represents his never-say-die attitude on the pitch, a trait that helped him earn a first senior call-up aged 18 and has kept him around the squad since. After backheeling in Côte d’Ivoire’s winning goal against Mali in the 2023 Africa Cup of Nations quarter-final on home soil, Diakité was sent off for a second yellow for taking his shirt off in celebration. He returned from suspension to come off the bench in the final against Nigeria.

data.name: Amad Diallo
data.description: Amad’s first major tournament with Côte d’Ivoire at the 2025 Africa Cup of Nations lived up to the hype. He started four games, winning the player of the match award in three of them, as he led Emerse Faé’s attack from the right wing. The quick-thinking dribbler already carries a lot of the responsibility when it comes to creating and scoring chances. Côte d’Ivoire know the 24-year-old Manchester United forward will have to be at his best if they are going to progress through the rounds this summer. “I’m not trying to be Côte d’Ivoire’s best player,” a humble Amad said during the last Afcon. “I am just trying to do my best.”

data.name: Mohamed Koné
data.description: Third-choice in between the posts, this will be another tournament for the 24-year-old to gain experience in the national setup. He has been around the senior side since 2024 after impressing for the youth teams but is yet to make an appearance (at the time of writing). With good reflexes and speed off his line, Koné has the attributes to become a regular one day. After going to the Africa Cup of Nations in December, he established himself as Charleroi’s No 1 in the final months of the Belgian season. Came through the Le Havre academy and made his professional debut in January 2024.

data.name: Guéla Doué
data.description: Ivorians always know Doué will give everything for the shirt. The former France youth international, who is the older brother of Paris Saint-Germain’s Désiré, chose to represent Côte d’Ivoire at under-23 level. “Since I was little, I always wanted to play for Côte d’Ivoire,” he says. “The orange shirt, the elephant. It’s my homeland. I’m proud to wear this shirt. I was really proud when I told my dad about it.” Emerse Faé’s Swiss Army knife, Doué is best at right-back but can more than fill in as a defensive midfielder. As effective going forward as in his defensive work, the 23-year-old showed his worth at his first Africa Cup of Nations in January and this World Cup could open doors for the Strasbourg player.

data.name: Ibrahim Sangaré
data.description: Called Little Yaya Touré when he was younger, the Nottingham Forest man can play in any position in midfield and has formed an effective partnership with Franck Kessié in the national team – it remains to be seen who else will join them in what is likely to be a midfield three. Sangaré’s qualities are largely in the defensive side of the game – tackling and haranguing opponents – but he is a good runner with and without the ball too. The 29-year-old has been part of the national setup since 2019 and acts as a go-between for the old stalwarts and the new generation. After a difficult early spell, including contracting malaria at the 2023 Africa Cup of Nations, Sangaré has settled in England and is one of Forest’s most consistent performers.

data.name: Nicolas Pépé
data.description: “The World Cup is a childhood dream,” Pépé said recently. “It’s the biggest competition in world football. I’m 30 now and I think this will be my only chance to play in one because there’s no guarantee I’ll be in the national team by the time I’m 34.” After playing at four Africa Cup of Nations and failing to qualify for two World Cups, the winger faces the challenge of his life. The skilled dribbler remains one of the key players of this Côte d’Ivoire side – and one of the loudest voices. A big presence in the dressing room, Pépé often shares videos of his teammates on social media. The former Arsenal winger is serious about playing for the national team too: “I have given my head for this team by shaving my mohican since under-17 level,” he said, a way of showing his respect for the badge.

data.name: Emmanuel Agbadou
data.description: The former Wolves defender has more power, physicality and technical ability than the average centre-back. Agbadou is the pastor of the national team and often carries messages about his relationship with God on a T-shirt under his matchday shirt. Badobre is his nickname – it means buffalo in Agbadou's native language, Bété – and he is still patiently awaiting his big breakthrough with the national team. His first cap came in 2020 but he is yet to establish himself as a starter and watched the 2023 Africa Cup of Nations triumph from home. This World Cup is an opportunity for him to become an international regular and build on an impressive second half of the season in Turkey.

data.name: Evan Ndicka
data.description: Ndicka had his pick of three national teams at the start of his career. Born in France to a Cameroonian father and an Ivorian mother, he eventually chose to represent Côte d’Ivoire in 2023 after playing for France all the way from under-15 to under-23 level. After making his first steps in football in the Parisian suburbs, the defender signed for Auxerre at 19. Reliable and reserved, on and off the pitch, Ndicka is a born leader. Known as the Minister of Defence, he has brought solidity to the Côte d’Ivoire backline thanks to his consistency. The only left-footed centre-back in the Ivorian squad is attracting interest from big clubs and will be in the shop window in North America this summer.

data.name: Evann Guessand
data.description: Guessand is often labelled as Faé’s teacher’s pet by the Ivorian press. The pair first worked together when Faé was his coach with Nice’s under-19s. Guessand earned his first Côte d’Ivoire call-up in 2024 and has become one of their first-choice attacking players. A quick, powerful forward who can play out wide or through the middle, he will have a battle on his hands to secure a starting place this summer. He did not hit the ground running after his €35m move to Aston Villa last summer and did not make much of an impact on loan at Crystal Palace since January.

data.name: Alban Lafont
data.description: Lafont’s first call-up last year came as a surprise to most because his links to Côte d’Ivoire were not well known. The goalkeeper had come close to a senior France call-up and could also have played for Burkina Faso, the country of his birth. Lafont’s mother, who represented Burkina Faso in handball and later became a member of parliament, had an Ivorian father. The 27-year-old joined up with Côte d’Ivoire as the second-choice but is now pushing Yahia Fofana hard for the starting spot. At 1.96m (6ft 5in) Lafont is confident when coming to claim crosses and his frame covers the goal well. Has already had a long and successful career with almost 300 Ligue 1 appearances with Toulouse and Nantes, winning the French Cup with the latter. He spent the 2025-26 season on loan in Greece.

data.name: Bazoumana Touré
data.description: Côte d’Ivoire’s TGV (high-speed train) is the second-youngest player on the plane to North America. The Hoffenheim forward is an old-fashioned winger – he is left-footed and plays off the left – with his main aim to set up teammates rather than shoot himself. The 20-year-old is mature for his age and capable of pulling the strings in midfield too. Pace is his main asset and that makes him a dangerous proposition off the bench against tiring defenders. Bazou, as he is known, may come across his club-mate Oliver Baumann in the Germany goal during the group stage (although chances of that receded when Manuel Neuer was called up). “He’s a club legend and like a mentor. He helps me with everything,” Touré said recently.

data.name: Parfait Guiagon
data.description: One of the squad’s creative sparks, Guiagon’s favoured position is in a traditional No 10 role, where he has the freedom to operate infield or drift out to the left wing. A silky dribbler who is most dangerous in gaps between the lines, the 25-year-old has the quality to dictate games when Côte d’Ivoire have more of the ball. After a series of trials – including at Barcelona and Monaco – he spent his first few years in Europe in Israel, most notably performing for Maccabi Tel Aviv, and now plays in Belgium. Called up by Emerse Faé for the first time last year, he is one of those going to their first major tournament this summer. Included in the Guardian’s Next Generation series as a 17-year-old in 2018.

data.name: Christ Inao Oulaï
data.description: Inao Oulaï was bullish after being handed his first senior call-up aged 19 last November. “If I manage to set foot on the pitch, I think the Ivorians will remember my name because I’m not going to think much about it: I’m just going to throw myself into it,” he said. This sums up exactly what Inao brings to the national team: risk-taking, energy and, most of all, not a care in the world. The midfielder is the face of the next generation of Côte d’Ivoire footballers and went to his first major tournament at the Africa Cup of Nations in January. He’s a contender to be the revelation of this World Cup, both within his national team and more widely.
